Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 11 - Defining Models

This chapter is a little shorter. It mostly covers the basics of defining a Model, but the thing is, in normal development you don't really do much setup on the Model side, so you'll notice this chapter genuinely doesn't have a whole lot to it.
Defining a Model is pretty simple, but before that we need to create the Model folder first. ThinkPHP doesn't give you a Model folder by default, so we'll create a
and add a new file called about.php:
And defining the Model only takes this much:
language-php shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

<?php
namespace app\about\model;

use think\Model;

class about extends Model
{
}
The one line you really need to watch out for in there is
. This tells ThinkPHP that we want to use the Model core library, and if you leave this line out, it won't run properly.
Okay, congrats, you've finished a basic Model definition (?!). But just defining a Model isn't enough — after all, a Model talks to the DB, so next we need to set up the database.
Once we've done the above, we need to wire up the database connection, otherwise the Model can't do anything with the DB.
The database config file lives at
. You just fill in the relevant fields:
Basically you only need to fill in
. Based on my MySQL setup, here's how I filled it in:
There's one extra field I filled in though —
. The database I created uses a prefix, so I set it to
Next we'll add a table (we'll use it in later chapters). I'll just hand you the SQL directly:
language-sql shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

CREATE TABLE think_user (
  id int(10) NOT NULL auto_increment,
  name varchar(5) NOT NULL default '',
  weight int(5) NOT NULL default 0,
  height float(5) NOT NULL default 0,
  PRIMARY KEY (id)
) AUTO_INCREMENT=1;
You'll notice my table name has a prefix on it too, which is going to help us out in the later operations. The main reason is that when I set up database.php earlier, I filled in
, so every table I create has to carry that prefix, otherwise ThinkPHP won't find it.
Pointing a Model at a table
for both the database and tables in database.php, when we point at a table next, we have to write it like this:

<?php
namespace app\about\model;

use think\Model;

class about extends Model
{
  protected $table = 'think_user';
}
You can also write the short form and let ThinkPHP tack on
for you — I just personally prefer writing it out in full:
And if you want this Model to connect to a different database, that's fine too. You just write in the settings to override the originals:

<?php
namespace app\about\model;

use think\Model;

class about extends Model
{
  protected $connection = [
    // database type
    'type' => 'mysql',
    // database IP
    'hostname' => '127.0.0.1',
    // database name
    'database' => 'test',
    // username
    'username' => 'root',
    // password
    'password' => '',
    // database port
    'hostport' => '',
    // params
    'params' => [],
    // charset
    'charset' => 'utf8',
    // prefix
    'prefix' => 'think_',
    // Debug mode
    'debug' => true,
    ];
}
Walking through it like this, honestly it's all pretty vague right now. So in the later chapters we'll gradually pair up Controller + Model operations to make the concepts stick.
